straight_beat.name: Marty Slutskoff
straight_beat.column:
The White Sox lost again. Not in a dramatic, heartbreaking way—no walk-off grand slam, no last-minute rally—but in the kind of fashion that makes you wonder if the team’s DNA has been altered by some cosmic error. Yesterday’s 6-5 defeat to the Royals wasn’t just another loss; it was a reminder of the South Side numbers that have haunted this club for years. Six wins, ten losses, and a 3.5-game gap in the AL Central. It’s the same old script, just with a different opponent.  

The pitching staff, once a source of pride, now feels like a collection of misfits. Corbin Burnett, supposed to be the backbone of the rotation, lasted just four innings yesterday, surrendering three runs. You’d think a guy with a 4.00 ERA would be able to navigate a Royals lineup that’s about as threatening as a Tuesday night trivia squad. But here we are, watching him struggle

optimist.name: Rosie Karamucka
optimist.column:
The game yesterday wasn’t just another loss in the standings—it was a reminder that the White Sox still have the grit to claw back. You think I’m worried about a 100-loss season? Honey, I survived the ’70s. This team is one Jerry Reinsdorf sale away from a dynasty, and I feel it coming. Sure, the record says 6-10, but that’s just the scoreboard. The real story is in the details: the way José Abreu swung that inside pitch for the go-ahead home run, the way Luis Robert chased that fly ball like it was a 1985 playoff game, and the way the bullpen held serve when it counted. You can’t measure heart in wins and losses, but you can see it in the way those guys keep showing up.  

I’ve got that rally towel in my purse, you know. Not for the 2005 World Series alone—it’s for every time the team’s been down and dug deep. That game in Kansas City? The way they rallied from a three-run deficit? It’s the same fire that burned in 2005. Back then, we were a bunch of underdogs with a lot of heart, and we proved it. Now? The same spirit’s still here, even if the results haven’t caught up yet. The front office is tinkering, the trades are happening, and the fans are still in the stands. You

pessimist.name: Len Cwiklinski
pessimist.column:
The White Sox beat the Royals yesterday, 6-5, but you won’t hear me celebrating. Not because the win is meaningless, but because it’s a mirage. A hundred and twenty losses is coming. You can feel it in the cheap hot dogs and the cheaper decisions. Reinsdorf should’ve sold in 2006 and retired with dignity.  

The team’s record—6-10, 3.5 games behind—feels like a punchline. You’d think a win would lift the mood, but no. The White Sox are a team in freefall, and the front office is the pilot who’s lost all sense
